coredata.dc:title: Boosting the electrochemical performance of MoS2 nanospheres-N-doped-GQDs-rGO three-dimensional nanostructure for energy storage and conversion applications
coredata.dc:creator: Sekar, Karthi
Raji, Govindan
Tong, Liangyu
Zhu, Yuanyuan
Liu, Shanhu
Xing, Ruimin
coredata.dc:description: The rational designed three-dimensional (3D) molybdenum-disulfide (MoS2) nanostructures have attracted significant research interest as electrode materials for energy conversion and storage applications. Herein, we developed 3D-MoS2 nanospheres and N-doped GQDS (N-GQDs) anchored with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanostructure (MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO, MQG) by facile hydrothermal method which was used as an electrode material for supercapacitor and electrochemical water splitting. TEM image reveals that the self-assembly of MoS2 nanospheres were anchored on the surface of rGO-nanosheets without aggregation. The BET surface area of the MQG sample (43.3 m2/g) was 8.8-fold greater than that of MoS2 (4.9 m2/g). The MQG electrode shows an excellent specific-capacitance of 416.5 F/g at 1 A/g which was greater than that of MoS2 (162.7 F/g). Also, 75.8% of its specific-capacitance was retained after 1000 cycles at 2 A/g, indicating its good stability. Moreover, the MQG nanostructure showed the preferable HER activity in 1.0 M KOH solution with a low overpotential of 145 mV at current density of 10 mA/cm2 compared with MoS2 (186 mV). The 3D-nanostructure could improve the conductivity, surface area, ion-diffusion and prevent structural collapse in the electrochemical reaction, further leading to superior electrochemical performance. These results clearly indicate the MQG nanostructure as a highly propitious electrode for supercapacitor and HER applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Three-dimensional
Nanostructures
Molybdenum-disulfide
Supercapacitor
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, (b and c) TEM, (d) HR-TEM images and (e) STEM images and corresponding elemental mapping of the of MQG sample.
XRD pattern of M, MQ, MG and MQG samples.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, high resolution XPS spectra of (b) Mo 3d, (c) S 2p, (d) N 1s, (e) C 1s and (f) O 1s of MQG sample.
Raman spectra of M and MQG samples.

(a) CV curves of rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes at 25 mV/s, (b) CV curves of MQG electrode at different scan rate (5–100 mV/s), (c) GCD curves of rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes at 1 A/g, (d) GCD curves of MQG electrode at different current densities (1–5 A/g).

(a) Rate performances of rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes at various current densities, (b) Nyquist plots of rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes, (c) The cyclic performance of MQG electrode at current density of 2 A/g, (d) Ragone plot for rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes.

(a) HER polarization curves of bare Ni foam, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes at a scan rate of 2 mV/s in 1.0 M KOH. (b) Tafel slopes for the M, MQ, MG and MQG catalysts. (c) Long-term durability of MQG for chronopotentiometry at current density of 25 mA/cm2 for 50 h. (d) Polarization curves of MQG at a scan rate of 2 mV/s before and after the chronopotentiometry test at 25 mA/cm2 for 50 h.
Schematic representation of synthesis strategy for MQG nanostructure.

head.ce:title: Boosting the electrochemical performance of MoS2 nanospheres-N-doped-GQDs-rGO three-dimensional nanostructure for energy storage and conversion applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 47504, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Chemistry
Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 47504, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational designed three-dimensional (3D) molybdenum-disulfide (MoS2) nanostructures have attracted significant research interest as electrode materials for energy conversion and storage applications. Herein, we developed 3D-MoS2 nanospheres and N-doped GQDS (N-GQDs) anchored with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanostructure (MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO, MQG) by facile hydrothermal method which was used as an electrode material for supercapacitor and electrochemical water splitting. TEM image reveals that the self-assembly of MoS2 nanospheres were anchored on the surface of rGO-nanosheets without aggregation. The BET surface area of the MQG sample (43.3 m2/g) was 8.8-fold greater than that of MoS2 (4.9 m2/g). The MQG electrode shows an excellent specific-capacitance of 416.5 F/g at 1 A/g which was greater than that of MoS2 (162.7 F/g). Also, 75.8% of its specific-capacitance was retained after 1000 cycles at 2 A/g, indicating its good stability. Moreover, the MQG nanostructure showed the preferable HER activity in 1.0 M KOH solution with a low overpotential of 145 mV at current density of 10 mA/cm2 compared with MoS2 (186 mV). The 3D-nanostructure could improve the conductivity, surface area, ion-diffusion and prevent structural collapse in the electrochemical reaction, further leading to superior electrochemical performance. These results clearly indicate the MQG nanostructure as a highly propitious electrode for supercapacitor and HER applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In worldwide continuous and extensive use of fossil fuels have led to fast depletion of fossil fuels, increased environmental pollution and global warming. To circumvent the energy crisis and environmental pollution problems, recently many researchers have contributed widespread attention to develop alternative sustainable and clean energy sources to supplement and replace the conventional fossil fuels [1–10]. Electrochemical energy harvesting and energy storage can serve as an important clean energy source for the future. Electrochemical capacitor (supercapacitor or ultracapacitor) and water splitting (hydrogen evolution reaction) are gaining a lot of attention in the last two decades owing to their high efficiency, low cost, eco-friendlyness and renewability.

In recent years, molybdenum-disulfide (MoS2) has been extensively explored as the active materials for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and supercapacitor due to their low cost, unique structural and superior electrochemical properties [11–18]. MoS2 nanostructures have an analogue of graphene with two-dimensional (2D) layered nanostructure which is composed of S-Mo-S layers under the weak van der Waals interaction along the c-axis [13,19]. The wide and weak van der Waals gap allows easy intercalation of foreign ions (Na+, H+, K+, etc.) for interlayer charge storage [20]. In addition, MoS2 has been reported that only the sulfur edge sites of MoS2 layers can enhance the HER catalytic activity, while their unexposed basal planes were inert to catalytic activity [21,22]. Despite recent great development of various MoS2 nanomaterials in supercapacitor and HER practical applications, the key scientific and technical challenges remained not to be overcome. For example, MoS2 shows poor conductivity, few accessible active sites, restacking of the layered interfaces and aggregations which hinders the charge storage and electrocatalytic activity.

In order to circumvent these problems, various carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene and activated carbon have been used as template to grow single and few layers of MoS2 with different morphologies like nanoflowers [23], nanobelts [24], nanosheets [25] and hollow nanospheres [26,27] to enhance the electrical conductivity, abundant active sites, ionic transport rates and inhibit the restacking. Among these aforementioned materials, graphene is one of the promising electrode material which shows many outstanding advantages including excellent electrical conductivity, high carrier mobility, high specific surface area and good chemical stability [28–30]. Moreover, three dimensional (3D) ternary nanostructure designed by graphene oxide (GO) with MoS2 and it can be used as an effective strategy to improving the conductance of active materials and rapid electron transport [31–33]. In such ternary nanostructures, 2D GO not only serve as a matrix to anchor the MoS2 layers and also improve the electrochemical performance of semiconducting MoS2. Recently, many MoS2 nanostructures grown on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as template showed the efficient electrochemical performance for supercapacitor, HER, lithium ion batteries and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [21,34–38]. In specific, the 2D nanosheets are assembled into 3D nanospheres which offers a hollow structure with a large surface area, high electrical conductivity and more active sites [36]. For example, hollow rGO nanospheres supported with ultrathin MoS2 nanosheet arrays fabricated by dual template method were used as electrode materials for supercapacitor and HER [39]. Wang et al. recently developed binder-free MoS2@CNT/rGO by vacuum-assisted filtration method and obtained a specific capacitance of 129 mF cm−2 at 0.1 mA cm−2 [40]. Liu et al. prepared 3D sandwiched MoS2/C@rGO nanostructure via supramolecular self-assembly methods and it delivered a specific capacitance of 340.0 F/g at 1 A/g [41]. Liu et al. reported hybrid MoS2/PEI-GO composites with a specific capacitance of 153.9 F/g at 1 A/g [42]. In addition, 3D MoS2-rGO-CNTs with layer-by-layer structure prepared by a vacuum filtration route was demonstrated to be an efficient electrocatalysts for HER [43].

However, during the MoS2/rGO formation, the structure of rGO was broken consequently affecting its electrical conductivity [44]. The above mentioned issue can be overcome by addition/functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) during MoS2/rGO formation which protects the structure of rGO [45]. GQDs is one of the carbon materials with similar structure of rGO and has been used as electrode material in the supercapacitor application owing its outstanding electrical conductivity, high surface area and good dispersion nature [46]. Moreover, GQDs has are very small particles with size about <10 nm which provide more oxygen containing active reaction sites on the surface than that of rGO [47]. In addition, doping of heteroatoms (boron, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorous, and sulfur) with GQDs would result in improved electrochemical performance which are essential for supercapacitor and HER [46,48]. Recently, coral-shaped MoS2 decorated with GQDs were prepared via chemical vapour deposition method which exhibits prominent electrocatalytic activity for HER [49].

In this work, we fabricated 3D MoS2 nanospheres and nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) anchored with rGO (MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO, MQG) nanostructure by a facile hydrothermal method. The morphological and physico-chemical properties of the prepared materials were characterized. Furthermore, the supercapacitor and HER performance of the MQG nanostructure were investigated in a three-electrode system. We observed that the MQG nanostructure enhanced supercapacitor and HER performance when compared to pristine MoS2. The results indicated that the prepared MQG nanostructure can serve as a competent electrode material and electrocatalyst for supercapacitor and HER applications, respectively.

Graphite flakes, citric acid dihydrate (C6H8O7·2H2O), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), ethanol, ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), thioacetamide (C2H5NS), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), Nafion solution, carbon black and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification.

Modified Hummer’s method was adopted for the preparation of GO from graphite flakes [50]. In brief, NaNO3 (1 g) and graphite flakes (2 g) was mixed with 46 mL of concentrated H2SO4 under stirring for 30 min. Then, 6 g of KMnO4 was added slowly under ice cold condition and then the mixture was stirred at 35 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, hot deionized (DI) water (172 mL) was added slowly and further stirred for 20 min. Appropriate amount of 3% H2O2 aqueous solution was added drop by drop into the reaction mixture with continuous stirring until the bubbles appearance. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with DI water for several times to remove the byproducts and the collected product was dried overnight at 60 °C.

N-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as starting precursors [51]. 1.68 g of C6H8O7·2H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of DI water and then 1.44 g of ethylenediamine was added and stirred for 30 min to form a homogenous solution. Afterward, the solution was transferred into 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation and dispersed in DI water.

To synthesis MQG nanostructures, 298 mg of C2H5NS and 214 mg of Na2MoO4·2H2O were dissolved in 40 mL DI water and stirred for 30 min. 40 mg of prepared GO was homogenously dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min and then above reaction mixture was added slowly into it and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of N-GQDs was added under vigorous stirring for 10 min. The resultant mixture was transferred into Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 24 h and then cooled down to room temperature naturally. The precipitate was obtained via centrifuging and washing several times with water and ethanol and then the product was dried overnight in 60 °C. Pristine MoS2 (M), MoS2-N-GQDS (MQ) and MoS2-rGO (MG) were also prepared by the same method for comparison. All the samples were heat treated in tube furnace at 400 °C for 3 h under N2 atmosphere.

The SEM images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100F scanning electron microscope operated at 2.0 kV. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared samples were obtained from Bruker D8 advance A25 powder diffractometer using CuKα radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping images were performed with a JEOL-2100 microscope operating at 200 kV. Raman spectra of the prepared samples were recorded using Renishaw in Via Raman spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of MQG sample were recorded using a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCA 3100, Shimadzu). The surface area and the pore size of the prepared catalysts were investigated by a Quantachrome Nova-1000 surface analyzer.

Electrochemical measurements of the prepared samples were tested using CHI660D electrochemical work station with 1 M Na2SO4 as electrolyte solution in a three electrode system by taking platinum wire and Ag/AgCl as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was modified by mixing of active material, carbon black as the conducting agent and PTFE as the binder in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 using an agate mortar and a few drops of ethanol was added into it to make homogenous slurry. The resulting slurry was coated on nickel foam substrate (1 × 1 cm2) and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The loading mass on the nickel foam for test is about 1 mg.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies were tested at different scan rates over a potential window from −0.8 to 0.2 V. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves were recorded at different current density within the potential range of −0.8 to 0.2 V. The cycle stability tests were performed by GCD measurements with a current density of 2 A/g for 1000 cycles. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with 5 mV amplitude at current open circuit voltage in the frequency range of 100 Hz to 100 kHz.

The specific capacitance of working electrodes are calculated from the CV curves according to the following formula:
(1)
C s
=
∫ I d V
v m Δ V
where ∫IdV is the area under the CV curve, ΔV is the voltage window, m is the mass of the electrode materials and ν is the scan rate.

The specific capacitance (Cs), energy density (E, Wh/kg) and power density (P, W/kg) of the prepared electrodes are calculated from the GCD curves at various discharge current densities according to the following equations:
(2)
C s
=
I
Δ t
m
Δ V
(3)
E =
1 2
·
C
Δ V
2
3.6
(4)
P =
3600
E
Δ t
where Cs, I, m, Δt and ΔV are the specific capacitance (F/g), discharge current (A), mass of the active material (g), the discharge time (s) and potential window (V), respectively.

The HER properties of the prepared samples were investigated using an electrochemical CHI660D work station with 1 M KOH as the electrolyte solution in a three electrode system by taking a platinum wire and Hg/HgO as the counter electrode and the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared by 2 mg of active material dispersed in 370 µL of ethanol and 30 µL of 5 wt% Nafion solution and it was ultrasonicated for 30 min to obtain a homogenous mixture. The mixture was drop-casted onto the nickel foam substrate (1 × 1 cm2) and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

MoS2 nanospheres and N-GQDs anchored with rGO nanostructure was fabricated by following steps as schematically shown in Scheme 1 . First, GO nanosheets were synthesized by modified Hummer’s method through oxidizing natural graphite powder using strong oxidizing agents (H2O2, H2SO4 and KMnO4). Secondly, N-GQDs were synthesized by facile hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. Finally, self-assembly of MoS2 nanospheres and N-GQDs anchored on rGO layer by simple hydrothermal method, forming the MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO (MQG) nanostructure. During the initial reaction period, the hydrophilic functional groups of GO surface were used as template and attracted with MoO4 2−. Thioacetamide act as both sulfur source and reducing agent in the hydrothermal process, in which thioacetamide released H2S reduce MoO4 2− into MoS2 and GO into rGO. Besides, the MoS2 nuclei interrelated with each other to form MoS2 nanosheets and further these nanosheets were self-assembled into MoS2 nanospheres on rGO sheets due to the prolonged reaction time [35,52]. Meanwhile, N-GQDS were uniformly dispersed and anchored within the entire hybrid structure.

The morphology of the prepared materials were investigated by SEM and TEM analysis. SEM and TEM images of GO are given in Fig. S1(a,b), which displays the typical crumpled and rippled morphology and sheet like 2D structure with single or very thin layers and high transparency. The smooth planar sheets with 2D GO structures served as a substrate for the nucleation and growth of MoS2 nanoparticles. The SEM image of pristine MoS2 is shown in Fig. S1c and it can be clearly seen that the pristine MoS2 exhibited the sphere like morphology which is made up of many nanosheets stacking with an average diameter around 700 nm. Fig. 1 a shows the SEM image of MQG sample, revealing that the MoS2 nanospheres were anchored to the surface of the rGO sheets. TEM image of MQG is shown in Fig. 1b, it is seen that many MoS2 nanospheres were homogeneously dispersed on the rGO surfaces without aggregation with the diameter of about 250 nm, suggesting that the MoS2 nanospheres tended to nucleate and grown around the functional groups on GO sheets because of the decreased nucleation energy barrier [35]. Functional group of GO sheets was presumed to anchor MoS2 nanospheres and also efficiently reduce the size and inhibits their aggregation and restacking of MoS2.

The magnified TEM image (Fig. 1c) reveals that the notable distinction between the edge and center of the nanospheres, an obvious evidence of the hollow structure feature. Highly wrinkled surfaces indicate that the hollow nanospheres mainly consisted of many loosely stacked MoS2 nanosheets. The high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of MQG (Fig. 1d) reveals the crystalline structure of MoS2 and the layers of MoS2 nanosheets range from three to five layers. The interlayer spacing of MoS2 nanosheet is in the range of 0.64–0.89 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of (0 0 2) planes of hexagonal MoS2, which is bigger than that of interlayer spacing of bulk MoS2 (0.61 nm) [53]. The expanded interlayer spacing was presumed due to the presence of rGO. N-GQDS were uniformly dispersed and anchored with the entire hybrid structure with the fringe spacing of about 0.21 nm. The STEM elemental mappings of C, N, Mo and S elements taken by EDX spectroscopy (Fig. 1e) reveal the MoS2 nanospheres and N-GQDs on rGO at the nanometer scale. Mo and S elements distribute homogenously in the nanosphere whereas N and C elements were distributed uniformly throughout the whole MQG structure which confirms the presence and uniform distribution of N-GQDs. The MoS2 spheres and N-GQDs anchored on rGO nanosheets enhance the interlayer spacing which favors increase in the ion storage and leads to better electrochemical properties in the supercapacitor and HER performance.

Fig. S2 shows XRD pattern of GO and rGO samples. GO shows the strong diffraction peak located at 11.56° which is attributed to the (0 0 1) plane of GO. The rGO sample exhibited a broad peak at around 25°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of rGO nanosheets. This is obvious evidence that the GO is reduced to rGO. XRD patterns of the M, MQ, MG and MQG are shown in Fig. 2 . The diffraction peaks (0 0 2), (1 0 0), (1 0 3) and (1 1 0) observed in the XRD patterns of the samples are well in agreement with the standard JCPDS card of MoS2 (JCPDS 17-0744). Furthermore, the weak diffraction of rGO is detected in the MG and MQG samples which confirm the presence of rGO in these samples. However, N-GQDs diffraction peaks could not be distinguished in the MQ and MGQ samples, due to the low content of N-GQDs in the samples. It is important to note that compared to pristine MoS2, the position of diffraction peaks shifted to a lower angle and intensity decreased for MQ, MG and MQG samples, which indicates that the incorporation of rGO and N-GQDs could effectively avoid the agglomeration of MoS2 nanosheets.

The chemical valence states on the surface of the MQG sample was analyzed by XPS. The wide range XPS spectrum of the MQG nanostructures is shown in Fig. 3 a, which reveals that the predominant peaks are in well accordance with the elements of Mo, S, N, C and O. The high resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d (Fig. 3b) shows that the two peaks at 228.1 and 231.3 eV was assigned to the Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2, respectively [54]. The binding energies of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 were located at 162.8 and 161.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c), which suggested that the S chemical state was negatively divalent [4]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 284.1, 284.4, 285.6 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to the CC, CO, CN and CO, respectively. The CN peak confirms the presence of N-GQDs into the material. The high resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) corresponds to the following nitrogen bonds including pyridinic-N (398.2 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.1 eV), and graphitic N (400.3 eV) [55]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows three peaks at 531, 532.1 and 533 eV, which are assigned to MoO, CO/OCN and CO, respectively.

Raman spectra of pristine MoS2 and MQG samples are shown in Fig. 4 . The characteristic bands of pristine MoS2 observed at 378 and 404 cm−1 correspond to the in-plane E1 2g and out-of-plane A1g vibrational modes, respectively. MQG nanostructure showed the similar vibrational modes to MoS2, but the wavenumber of E1 2g and A1g vibrational mode in MQG nanostructures are slightly red shifted when compared to pristine MoS2, due to the presence of fewer-layer MoS2 nanosheets, as a result of the broken restacking of MoS2 flakes [56]. Besides, another two peaks at 1355 and 1588 cm−1 were also observed, corresponding to the D and G band of rGO, respectively [41]. The D band vibration corresponds to the defects and disorder in carbon materials whereas the G band is related to the graphitic sp2 hybridized carbons. In conclusion, the MoS2 nanospheres and N-GQDs are successfully anchored on rGO nanosheets through the hydrothermal process [54].

Further, the surface area and porosity of M and MQG samples were investigated by measuring the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of M and MQG (Fig. S3) show a type IV isotherm curve, indicating mesoporous nature of the samples. The BET surface area of MQG (43.3 m2/g) was 8.8-fold higher than that of pristine MoS2 (4.9 m2/g). Their corresponding average pore sizes were found to be 3.5 and 3.8 nm for pristine MoS2 and MQG, respectively. This result clearly shows that MoS2 nanospheres grown on the rGO sheet effectively enhanced the pore size and active surface area of the electrode, since the rGO nanosheets and N-GQDs inhibit the agglomeration and restacking of MoS2. The high surface area and pores of MQG would provide more catalytic active sites, larger contact area and shortened distance for charge transfer [57].

The electrochemical performance of the rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG samples were investigated through CV and GCD method are shown in Fig. 5 . The quasi-rectangular shape without any redox peaks occurred in all the CV curves of different samples at a scan rate of 25 mV/s between the potential window of −0.8 and 0.2 V, which indicates that the typical characteristic of electric double layer capacitance (EDLC) and the pseudocapacitive nature of the MoS2. MQG exhibited a higher current density and larger surface area than that of all other samples, implying that MQG possessed a higher specific capacitance. The specific capacitance of the GO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes were calculated from the CV curve based on Eq. (1) and their corresponding values are 39, 70, 81, 238 and 314 F/g, respectively at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The MQG electrode exhibit higher specific capacitance at identical scan rate than those of other sample due to the synergistic effect of MoS2, N-GQDs and rGO. The synergistic effect improved contact resistance and contact area between electrolyte and electrode which accelerated the charge diffusion during the electrochemical process [41]. Fig. 5b displays the CV curves of MQG at different scan rates (5–100 mV/s). The current density and area increased with increasing the scan rates, nevertheless the shape of the CV curve did not change significantly, indicating the ideal capacitive behavior and excellent charge collection in the MQG electrode.

Fig. 5c shows the GCD curves of different samples obtained at a potential range from −0.8 to 0.2 V at a current density of 1 A/g. All the samples show a quasi-triangular shape with a small internal resistance (IR) drop in discharge counterpart, which indicates the combination of EDLC-pseudo-capacitance behavior of the devices. The specific capacitance (Cs) of rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG samples are calculated from Eq. (2) to be 72, 162.7, 219.5, 359.6 and 416.5 F/g, respectively. The specific capacitance of the MQG-modified electrode was higher than that of all other electrodes, resulting from the enlarged surface area and high conductivity which facilitates more electrochemical active sites for the transfer of charges. The GCD curves of the MQG-modified electrode at different current densities are shown in Fig. 5d. The specific capacitance decreased with increasing the current density for all the samples, which is due to the deficit of time to intercalate the active species over the electrode materials at higher current density (Fig. 6 a). Impressively, the MQG-modified electrode revealed better cyclic stability at higher current density with 74.6% of capacitance retention at 5 A/g. The specific capacitance of the prepared MQG electrode and recently reported MoS2-based electrodes are summarized in Table S1, The present MQG electrode shows the high specific capacitance when compared to the previously reported electrode materials, confirming that the presence of rGO and N-GQDs into MoS2 which could remarkably boost its electrochemical storage properties.

EIS measurement was used to determine the internal resistance and to investigate charge transfer kinetics and ion diffusion process of the electrodes. Fig. 6b shows the impedance Nyquist plots of the prepared electrodes at an open circuit potential which composed of one semicircle at high frequency region and a linear line at low frequency region. The depressed semicircle and straight line of Nyquist curve corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and semi-diffusion process. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the rGO, M, MQ, MG and MQG electrodes was calculated by fitting the equivalent circuit to be 0.03, 1.31, 0.94, 0.24 and 0.04 Ω, respectively. The MQG sample gave a low Rct value and more straight line in low frequency region when compared to pristine MoS2, which indicates the fast charge transfer rates and high conductivity. So, it should be concluded that rGO and N-GQDs supporter play a key role in decreasing the charge transfer resistance.

The energy density, power density and cycling stability are essential parameters for evaluating the supercapacitor performance of the prepared electrode. Fig. 6c shows the cycling stability of the MQG electrode over 1000 cycles between −0.8 and 0.2 V at a current density of 2 A/g. Though, the specific capacitance continuously decreases with increasing the number of cycles, there is still 75.8% retention of the initial capacitance. The energy density and power density of the prepared electrodes were derived from Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. From these plots in Fig. 6d, the energy density increased with decreasing the power density for all the samples. The energy density of MQG electrode is found to be 57.8 Wh/Kg at a power density of 500 W/Kg which is higher than that of the pristine MoS2 electrode (22.6 Wh/Kg at 500 W/Kg).

MQG electrode shows better electrochemical performance than the other prepared electrodes which was confirmed by the CV, GCD and EIS data. The synergetic effect of MoS2, GO and N-GQDs intensely increase the capacitor performance, due to the easy intercalation of ions via the Van der Waals gap of rGO and MoS2 layers. The presence of rGO in the hybrid material may inhibits the agglomeration of the MoS2 nanosheets [4]. The presence of N-GQDs in hybrid structure is favorable to enhance the electrochemical performance and conductivity on the electrode material due to the lone pair electron for conjugation with the p-conjugated rings and the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the graphene layer [58]. Therefore, the MQG nanostructure possessed more active sites towards the accessibility of electrolyte ion and thereby enhanced the capacitance behavior of the MQG material.

The HER performance of the prepared catalysts were investigated by the polarization curves from linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte with a scan rate of 2 mV s−1. Fig. 7 a represents the corresponding polarization curves of the prepared catalyst, it is noticeable to see that the MQG catalyst exhibits a low overpotential of 145 mV to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm2 while the nickel foam has a negligible activity towards HER in 1.0 M KOH. Nevertheless, M, MQ and MG catalysts show overpotentials of 186, 169 and 154 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, respectively. MQG catalyst shows lower overpotential value than those of other catalysts owing to the synergistic effect of rGO, N-GQDs and MoS2. Moreover, the MQG electrode exhibited superior HER performance compared to the previously reported h-rGO@MoS2 architecture (230 mV) [39], MoS2-rGO hybrid (160 mV) [4] and MoS2-rGO composite (168 mV) [59].

The electrochemical HER reaction kinetics of the prepared catalysts were investigated via the Tafel slope, which is determined by fitting the linear portions of the Tafel plots to the Tafel equation (η = a + blog(j) , where η is the overpotential, a is the Tafel constant, b is the Tafel slope and j is the current density). Tafel plots of the prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 7b. The Tafel slope of M, MQ, MG and MQG catalysts were determined to be 119.8, 104, 66.7 and 53 mV dec−1, respectively. The MQG catalyst exhibits a lower Tafel slope when compared to the other prepared catalysts, due to the strong electronic and chemical interaction between rGO, N-GQDs and MoS2 and it provides more catalytic edge sites. The Tafel slope of MQG catalyst falls within the range of 40–120 mV dec−1, which indicate that the electrochemical HER reaction proceeds through the Volmer - Heyrovsky mechanism [60].

The long-term stability of MQG catalyst was investigated by chronopotentiometry (η-t) measurement with constant current density of 25 mA/cm2 for 50 h which shown in Fig. 7c. It is clear that the MQG catalyst exhibited a continuous HER behavior for 50 h. After a period of 50 h, the negligible degradation of current density was observed, a supportive evidence for excellent stability for long term operation. Besides, after the chronopotentiometry measurement, the polarization curve showed a small ignorable shift from the initial one (Fig. 7d). These results confirm that the long-term electrochemical stability of the MQG catalyst during the HER process in the alkaline electrolyte. The superior catalytic performance of MQG catalyst for HER could be attributed to the following aspects: (i) ultrathin rGO nanosheets and ultra-small-sized N-GQDs improved the conductivity of MoS2; (ii) MoS2 nanosheets had an expanded interlayer spacing which could favor the diffusion kinetics of ions; (iii) the hollow structure of MoS2 nanospheres provided a large number of exposed active edge sites and increased contact area with the electrolyte [27,36,37]. Therefore, the synergistic effects of MoS2, N-GQDs and rGO enhanced the intercalation and transfer process of ions and prevented the layered stacking but with the exposure of additional active edge sites [35].

In summary, 3D MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO (MQG) nanostructure has been fabricated to achieve the integration of large surface area and high conductivity to collectively improve the electrocatalytic performance for supercapacitor and HER applications. In this process, MoS2 nanospheres and N-GQDs were anchored on rGO nanosheets, which efficiently prevented the restacking of MoS2. Compared to pristine MoS2-modified electrode (162.7 F/g), the MQG-modified electrode exhibited a higher specific capacitance of 416.5 F/g at 1 A/g; also, it exhibited superior cycling stability with the capacitance retention of 75.8% at current density 2 A/g after 1000 cycles and remained a high rate capability (310.7 F/g at 5 A/g). As for MQG as HER electrocatalyst in 1 M KOH, a lower overpotential of 145 mV was enough to drive a current density of 10 mA/cm2 with an attractive Tafel slop of 53 mV dec−1. The MoS2-N-GQDs-rGO nanostructure will be a competent electrode material as well as electrocatalyst for both supercapacitor and HER applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21950410531), and Science & Technology Research Project of Henan province (182102410090). Also we thank Dr. Daibing Luo from Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the discussion and analysis.
